title: Why Kazakhstan Is Changing Where Its History Begins
gist: Kazakhstan is rewriting where its national history begins, reclaiming the Golden Horde as its own lineage and downgrading the significance of 1991 independence, a shift from the civic-nationhood model it adopted when ethnic Kazakhs were only 39.7% of the population.

insights.claim: Kazakhstan is revising its national origin story not by rejecting 1991 independence but by making it considerably less significant, reclaiming the Golden Horde as direct lineage rather than foreign conquering history.
insights.speaker: Issatay Minuarov
insights.so_what: A shift toward ethno-nationalist historiography signals evolving identity politics that can affect Russia relations and domestic cohesion.
insights.locator: Kazakhstan is now revising that explanation

insights.claim: Kazakhstan's first major post-Soviet history, published in 1997, reproduced the Soviet paradigm almost unchanged, treating the Golden Horde as foreign history with no direct lineage to Kazakh civilization.
insights.speaker: Issatay Minuarov
insights.so_what: Shows how recently the Soviet-inherited framing dominated, underscoring the significance of the current break.

insights.claim: At independence in 1991, ethnic Kazakhs were just 39.7 percent of the population and ethnic Russians 37.8 percent, making Kazakhstan the only Soviet republic to enter independence without its titular nationality forming a majority.
insights.speaker: Issatay Minuarov
insights.so_what: The demographic near-parity explains why a civic (not ethnic) nationhood model was originally necessary and why the current revision is now feasible.
insights.locator: At independence, ethnic Kazakhs accounted for just 39.7 percent

insights.claim: Under 1990s conditions, a civic nationhood model was the most practical means of building Kazakhstan's state and preventing fragmentation in a diverse society with deep linguistic divisions.
insights.speaker: Issatay Minuarov
insights.so_what: The move away from civic nationhood now implies Astana feels demographically secure enough to assert a more ethnic identity.
